Title: #NSTviral: Remember petition writers? This 87-year-old is keeping the tradition alive [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: An 87-year-old "uncle" has warmed hearts online after a video of him patiently helping members of the public fill out forms and write letters at a government office near Jalan Duta went viral.
The service, commonly known as petition writing, was once a familiar sight near government offices, where members of the public sought help to complete official forms, letters and documents.
Threads user alyadanielmaleky shared that she had accompanied her mother to replace her lost passport.
She said her mother, who had suffered a stroke, had not walked much for some time, making the process more challenging. With several procedures to complete, she sought help from the elderly man, who was stationed nearby, to fill out the forms.
"The form-filling part, we asked the uncle to help. It was very neat and detailed. Thank you, uncle.
"He is 87 years old but still strong at work," she wrote.
She later learned from the man's friend that he had previously served as an officer with the National Registration Department.
"Thank you for making things easier for us," she added.
The video showed the elderly man focused at his station as he went through the paperwork carefully. A sign on his table also listed the services he offered.
The post has since received over 3,000 likes, with many praising the man's sharpness, discipline and work ethic.
Many commenters said the man represented a fading generation of workers whose patience, handwriting and attention to detail continue to leave a mark on those they help.
User khogwong said it was impressive that the man remained mentally and physically active in his 80s, while linda_abdrazak marvelled at his eyesight, noting that he did not appear to need glasses.
Others said the scene reminded them of a once-common sight.
User mamagugugagaa said the video brought back childhood memories.
"When I was little, I often saw elderly men sitting outside post offices with typewriters. Seeing this uncle is truly nostalgic," the user wrote.
Another user said the heartwarming post had lifted her spirits.
"After seeing this post, I immediately went into positive mode," wrote misjaja85.
View on Threads
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sarawak Oil Palms, Hibiscus poised to extend rally, says RHB Research
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sarawak Oil Palms Bhd and Hibiscus Petroleum Bhd could extend their recent rallies after breaking above key technical resistance levels, with RHB Research expecting further upside for both counters if buying momentum is sustained.
Sarawak Oil Palms emerged as one of the research firm's top technical picks after the plantation stock broke above the RM4.85 resistance level on strong trading volume, signalling continued bullish sentiment.
RHB Research analyst Aiman Kamil Ahmad Shauqi said the stock formed a higher-high white candlestick and closed above the resistance level, reinforcing the positive technical setup.
If the momentum persists, the stock could climb towards the next resistance levels of RM5.15 and RM5.35.
However, a decline below the RM4.65 support level would invalidate the bullish outlook and increase the likelihood of a price correction, he said.
Sarawak Oil Palms, which engages in the cultivation, processing, refining, and trading of palm products, has gained about 30.4 per cent so far this year. The counter rose from RM3.75 on Jan 2 to RM4.89 at Tuesday's close, giving the company a market capitalisation of about RM4.45 billion.
RHB Research also maintained a positive technical view on Hibiscus Petroleum, saying the oil and gas producer could see further gains after breaking above the RM1.85 resistance level.
The breakout signals strengthening buying interest and may pave the way for the stock to advance towards RM2.00, followed by the next resistance at RM2.15, the research house said.
It added that the positive technical outlook would be negated if the stock falls below the RM1.70 support level, which could trigger a fresh round of selling pressure.
Hibiscus Petroleum, Malaysia's first listed independent oil and gas exploration and production company, has risen about 25.2 per cent year-to-date, climbing from RM1.51 on Jan 2 to RM1.89 at Tuesday's close, valuing the company at about RM1.39 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM114mil from 'Sabah Watergate' seizure still with MACC
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The RM114 million in cash and assets seized in 2016 as part of the probe into a former Sabah Water Department director remains in the custody of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said seized assets were normally held by enforcement agencies until the court process was concluded.
She said the assets would only be forfeited and transferred to the Malaysian Treasury once a forfeiture order was secured from the court.
"During proceedings at the Sessions Court, the prosecution applied for all seized cash and property to be forfeited to the government.
"However, the court ordered the forfeiture application to be stayed pending the completion of all appeal processes in the higher courts," Azalina said.
"The prosecution filed an appeal at the High Court against the entire Sessions Court decision, and the matter remains subject to ongoing legal processes."
She said this in a written parliamentary reply to Datuk Seri Madius Tangau (GRS-Tuaran), who asked about the status of the RM114 million seized by the MACC in the probe.
In May, former Sabah Water Department director
Ag Mohd Tahir Mohd Talib
was sentenced to a total of 96 years' jail for 12 counts of money laundering in the case.
He was also fined RM284.3 million, in default of 78 months' jail.
The court, however, allowed Ag Mohd Tahir's application for a stay of execution pending appeal.
The case dominated headlines in 2016 after the MACC carried out one of its largest-ever seizures in Sabah, involving millions of ringgit in cash and luxury items.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: ICE agent fatally shoots Mexican motorist in Texas during vehicle stop
Article:
HOUSTON: A US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent fatally shot a motorist in Houston on Tuesday while officers were trying to stop the man's vehicle, the agency said, amid an escalating federal crackdown on migrants.
The man killed was identified as Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, whom ICE described as a Mexican national and "illegal alien" who attempted to evade arrest during a "targeted enforcement operation" by federal immigration officers.
Ronaldo Salgado, who identified himself as the slain motorist's son, told Spanish-language television station Telemundo Houston that his father was shot while he was seeking workers for hire in the area.
According to ICE's account of the incident, Salgado "rammed an ICE law enforcement vehicle, refused to follow multiple verbal commands, and weaponized his vehicle in an attempt to run over an ICE law enforcement officer".
The confrontation resulted in "our officer firing his weapon in self-defence", striking the driver, who was taken to hospital where he died of his injuries, ICE said.
Reuters could not immediately verify the man's immigration status or the circumstances of the shooting.
Video footage captured by a surveillance camera from a nearby business and reviewed by Reuters showed a person lying on the ground beside a white van and surrounded by officers, appearing to show the aftermath of the shooting.
Houston Fire Department spokesperson Rustin Rawlings said firefighters dispatched to the scene found a man who had been shot in the abdomen undergoing cardiopulmonary resuscitation before he was taken to a hospital.
CALLS FOR INDEPENDENT INQUIRY
In several instances over the past year, initial statements from immigration enforcement agencies about their use of force have been challenged by video footage or other evidence, sometimes in court.
In October, a Chicago-area woman, Marimar Martinez, was accused of ramming law enforcement officers with her car. She was shot five times but survived. Charges against her were later dropped, and video evidence suggested the agents may have struck her vehicle themselves.
Trump administration officials also said that two US citizens shot dead by federal agents on the streets of Minneapolis in January, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, had threatened bodily harm to the agents before they were killed, despite video evidence to the contrary.
In May, a Minnesota prosecutor charged an ICE agent with assaulting a Venezuelan man in a non-fatal shooting in Minneapolis during the same immigration crackdown in which Good and Pretti were killed.
ICE said in its statement on Tuesday that the FBI would lead the investigation into the shooting. The FBI did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
The deadly confrontation in Houston came amid a recent increase in ICE arrests nationwide, with immigration officers picking up around 2,000 migrants a day last week, according to two people familiar with the matter.
US Representative Sylvia Garcia, who represents the neighbourhood where the shooting took place, called for a "full and impartial investigation", adding that "the victim's family, my constituents, and the entire community deserve a complete and transparent accounting of what happened".
The call for an independent inquiry was echoed by Roman Palomares, president of the League of United Latin American Citizens.
"We have seen a pattern of ICE involvement in shootings and excessive use of force," he told reporters on Tuesday. "Each time a family is left without answers, and a community is left in fear."
Mary Benton, a spokesperson for Houston Mayor John Whitmire, said in a written statement that Tuesday's incident stemmed from "a federal operation" and that Houston police "were not involved".
Asked by Reuters if police were investigating the incident, Jodi Silva, a spokesperson for the Houston Police Department, said "to my understanding, no".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt to ensure sufficient petrol levels until December
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government will continue to ensure there are secure and sufficient levels of petroleum from July 2026 to December 2026, according to the Economy Ministry.
It said the country's oil and fuel supplies remained stable and sufficient to meet domestic demand as of June 2026, following the government's proactive measures implemented in phases since disruptions began in the Strait of Hormuz on Feb 28, 2026.
"Brent crude oil prices, which peaked at US$144.50 per barrel in early April 2026, declined to US$99.29 per barrel between June 1 and 5, 2026, reflecting increasingly positive market sentiment following progress in peace negotiations.
"The government is, however, not taking the situation lightly, as physical supply constraints and declining global inventories persist," it said in a written reply on the Dewan Rakyat website today.
The ministry was responding to a question from Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin (PN-Larut) on the current status of the country's oil and fuel supply security from July 2026 to December 2026, including projections of national petroleum stocks and the government's mitigation measures in response to global geopolitical conflicts.
Supply security is being ensured via a combination of three key approaches: diversifying import sources, optimising domestic fuel production through biodiesel, and implementing long-term supply agreements.
"The government has implemented several measures to safeguard oil supply security, including diversifying petroleum supply sources, optimising domestic fuel supply through biodiesel, preventing leakages through enforcement, and carrying out data-driven monitoring," it said. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against major currencies, easier versus US dollar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened higher against major currencies but eased against the US dollar on Wednesday as demand for the greenback strengthened amid escalating geopolitical tensions in West Asia.
At 8am, the local currency weakened to 4.0745/0830 against the US dollar from Tuesday's close of 4.0685/0735.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said global financial markets were expected to remain in risk-off mode following US military strikes against Iranian forces and the US Treasury Department's decision to revoke a waiver on Iranian oil sales.
"This suggests the ceasefire remains fragile, prompting a 6.13 per cent jump in Brent crude prices to US$76.40 per barrel," he told Bernama.
At the opening, the ringgit strengthened against a basket of major currencies.
It rose against the Japanese yen to 2.5092/5146 from 2.5179/5221 at Tuesday's close, appreciated against the British pound to 5.4378/4492 from 5.4668/4755, and gained against the euro to 4.6453/6550 from 4.6696/6771.
The local currency also traded higher against most regional currencies.
It strengthened against the Singapore dollar to 3.1495/1563 from 3.1593/1648 at Tuesday's close, appreciated against the Indonesian rupiah to 226.6/227.1 from 226.8/227.2, and gained against the Thai baht to 12.1965/2278 from 12.2545/2807.
It was unchanged against the Philippine peso at 6.63/6.65.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azalina: RCI decision depends on probe findings, public interest
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Any decision to establish a Royal Commission of Inquiry (RCI) into allegations of a corporate mafia within the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) will be considered based on legal procedures, ongoing investigations and the findings of the relevant authorities.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the government remained open to establishing an RCI or any other appropriate mechanism if deemed necessary in the public interest.
She said the government took seriously allegations involving the corporate mafia issue and matters that could undermine public confidence in government institutions.
"At present, investigations by the relevant authorities are ongoing to examine all allegations and establish complete and verified facts.
"It is important that these investigations are given the necessary space to be conducted independently, transparently and comprehensively in accordance with the law and established procedures," she said in a written parliamentary reply today.
Azalina was responding to Lim Guan Eng (PH-Bagan), who asked why an RCI had not been established to investigate former MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki's share ownership involving holdings allegedly exceeding the limit permitted for public officers, as well as allegations of a corporate mafia within the MACC.
Azalina said any follow-up action would be guided by the findings of the investigations to ensure every decision taken was based on facts, justice and the principles of the rule of law.
She said the establishment of an RCI was subject to the provisions of the Commissions of Enquiry Act 1950.
"Based on the standard operating procedures, the process of establishing an RCI begins with the preparation of a cabinet memorandum by the lead ministry to address matters of serious public interest.
"Upon approval by the cabinet, the prime minister will submit the proposal for the consideration and consent of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong.
"If consent is granted, the establishment of the RCI will involve determining its terms of reference, membership and duration of inquiry, which will be finalised through publication in the Federal Government Gazette before the commission begins its functions," she said.
Azalina said RCI proceedings would also be governed by the provisions of the Commissions of Enquiry Act 1950, and the report prepared must first be submitted to the Yang di-Pertuan Agong before undergoing further formalities, including presentation to the cabinet.
On June 30,
51 non-governmental organisations
called on the government to initiate an RCI into the corporate mafia scandal allegedly linked to the MACC and Azam.
The memorandum said the RCI must investigate Azam's personal involvement in the operations of the corporate mafia, including his alleged relationship with Andy Lim, who was allegedly involved.
It also asked the RCI to look into the complicity of the MACC's "Section D" wing in harassing and intimidating businesspeople to facilitate forced corporate takeovers, as well as Azam's personal involvement or complicity in the operations of the agency's 'Section D' wing, among other matters.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MyDigital ID expands to 19 state-level platforms
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: MyDigital ID is being expanded to state-level platforms, enabling access to online services provided by local authorities, Land and Mines Offices and state agencies.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the status of MyDigital ID integration at the state government level is a standing agenda item at State Digital Economy and Fourth Industrial Revolution Council meetings to ensure comprehensive and effective implementation.
"To date, 19 online applications under state governments have been integrated with MyDigital ID, while 28 applications remain in the development phase.
"The MyDigital ID system architecture is one of the key factors enabling government agencies to carry out integration quickly and easily without requiring additional hardware and software procurement.
"The government will ensure the MyDigital ID system remains available at an optimal level to enable various government online service systems to be accessed seamlessly," he said in a written parliamentary reply today.
He was responding to Wong Shu Qi (PH–Kluang), who asked for the number of MyDigital ID registrations as of May 31, as well as measures taken by the government to ensure all online government service platforms and systems could be accessed using MyDigital ID.
Zahid said 12 million Malaysians had registered for MyDigital ID, while 16 million registrations had been recorded, including renewal and cancellation transactions, as of June 30.
He said the government had taken several strategic measures to ensure government online platforms could be accessed using MyDigital ID as a digital identity authentication mechanism.
"A single sign-on implementation directive was issued by the Chief Secretary to the Government through a letter dated Jan 14, 2025, to all ministry secretaries-general, federal department heads, federal statutory bodies, state secretaries and local authorities.
"As of June 25, a total of 114 government online services had been integrated with MyDigital ID," he said.
Among the key government services that have adopted MyDigital ID as a single sign-on mechanism are MyJPJ, MyPTPTN, SPA9 and MyBayar PDRM.
Zahid added that MyDigital ID had also been integrated with MyGOV Malaysia, developed by the National Digital Department.
He said the integration of MyDigital ID as a single sign-on mechanism for users' digital identity authentication would further expand its use across government online service systems.
From: mia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Body of Iran's Khamenei arrives in Iraq ahead of funeral procession
Article:
NAJAF: The body of Iran's supreme leader Ali Khamenei arrived at Najaf International Airport in Iraq on Tuesday night, Iraqi state media reported, ahead of funeral processions in the country's holy cities that are home to shrines revered by Shia Muslims.
Iran began six days of public funeral ceremonies for Khamenei on Saturday, including a dedicated day in neighbouring Iraq, a Shia powerhouse with close ties to Tehran.
Khamenei, who was killed on the first day of the Middle East war in US-Israeli strikes, will be buried on Thursday in his hometown of Mashhad in northeast Iran.
Iraqi state TV broadcast the arrival of the Mahan Air flight that brought Khamenei's coffin to Najaf airport, where Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian had arrived shortly before to attend the official Iraqi ceremony.
Iraqi officials and senior politicians, including Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi, were also present to receive the remains of the late supreme leader.
Iranian commander Esmail Qaani, who heads the foreign operations branch of Iran's Revolutionary Guards, known as the Quds Force, was also present.
[[nid:1483239]]
At the airport, red carpets stretched across the terminal floor, with large posters displaying Islamic calligraphy and a portrait of Khamenei.
The Iraqi government declared Wednesday a public holiday, with processions set to begin at 6am (0300 GMT) in the holy city of Najaf.
In Najaf, mourners began arriving at least a day in advance for a six-kilometre procession that is expected to culminate at the majestic shrine of Imam Ali – the Prophet Mohammed's son-in-law and the first Shia Imam.
The city's main streets have been draped with welcoming banners and large portraits of the late leader.
Mohammed al-Bayati, 30, who travelled for hours to Najaf, told AFP "this is an opportunity that cannot be missed".
He added that he came to attend "the funeral of the figure who challenged the power of America and Israel".
After Najaf, Khamenei's body will be flown to Karbala, around 60 kilometres north, for another procession ending at the shrines of Imam Hussein and his brother Abbas.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gold slips as Middle East tensions lift oil prices, Fed minutes awaited
Article:
NEW YORK: Gold fell on Tuesday as investors monitored escalating hostilities in the Middle East that sent oil prices higher, while awaiting minutes from the US Federal Reserve's June meeting for guidance on the monetary policy outlook.
Spot gold was down 0.5 per cent at US$4,144.36 per ounce by 2pm ET (1800 GMT).
US gold futures for August delivery settled 0.3 per cent lower at US$4,157.40 an ounce.
Bullion hit a two-week high on Monday after weaker-than-expected US jobs data last week prompted markets to scale back expectations of a near-term interest rate hike.
However, "I think the reality is setting in that the Fed is still very much focused on reigning in inflation - so higher for longer still seems the most likely Fed path," said Peter Grant, vice-president and senior metals strategist at Zaner Metals.
Traders still expect a 60 per cent chance of a rate hike in September, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.
Attention is now turning to the Fed's meeting minutes, scheduled for release on Wednesday.
In the Middle East, two tankers were hit in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran said there would be no more peace talks unless US President Donald Trump halted his repeated threats to restart the war.
Oil prices edged higher following the news, keeping inflation concerns alive as fuel prices rose.
Gold often comes under pressure when inflation concerns keep interest rates elevated, reducing the appeal of non-yielding assets such as bullion.
Meanwhile, China's central bank maintained gold purchases for a 20th straight month, with its reserves reaching 75.44 million fine troy ounces by the end of June, up from 74.96 million a month earlier.
Hong Kong launched a central clearing system for gold on Tuesday and also revived gold futures trading as it seeks to become a regional reserve hub for bullion.
Among other metals, spot silver dropped 1.7 per cent to US$61 per ounce.
Platinum rose 1.2 per cent to US$1,651.25, while palladium gained 0.9 per cent to US$1,279.69.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil jumps and bonds dip as US strikes Iran
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil prices jumped and bond futures slid on Wednesday after the US struck Iran and reinstated trade sanctions following attacks on tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, while stocks wobbled as momentum ebbed from the record-breaking AI rally.
US crude futures were up 2.7 per cent at US$72.40 a barrel and 10-year Treasury futures slid seven ticks as traders priced in the risk of higher inflation and interest rates.
"Obviously the market doesn't like these attacks... but it's not full-blown panic mode," said Jason Wong, senior strategist at BNZ in Wellington.
The past few months showed the oil market's resilience to a huge supply shock, he said, although the vulnerability now is low global reserves.
Data this week showed stocks of crude in the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve had fallen to their lowest level since 1983.
Nikkei futures pointed to a fall in Japanese stocks, while S&P 500 futures were down about 0.1 per cent.
Wall Street indexes fell overnight after a sharp drop in Samsung Electronics shares, despite blockbuster earnings, showed investors were wary of extending a rally that has lifted South Korea's chipmaker-heavy market 82 per cent this year.
The US strikes are the latest challenge to last month's ceasefire and targeted air defences, coastal surveillance, anti-ship positions and drone launch sites, a US official told Reuters.
Washington also moved to withdraw a concession allowing Iran to sell oil on the global market, which Iran's foreign ministry said breached the framework agreement to end the war.
In currency markets, the dollar, which had eased from recent highs, remained firm and pushed the euro back to just above US$1.14 and the Australian dollar to US$0.6925.
Later on Wednesday, the Reserve Bank of New Zealand is due to set interest rates, with markets pricing in about an 85 per cent chance of a hike and most economists also forecasting an increase.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dollar at week-high after US resumes attacks on Iran
Article:
SINGAPORE: The dollar clung to its highest levels of the week against most of its peers at the start of trading in Asia on Wednesday as the US renewed strikes on Iran, rekindling geopolitical tensions and sending oil prices higher.
The dollar index, which measures the greenback's strength against a basket of six currencies, traded at 101.18, its highest level since July 2.
Demand for the safe-haven currency came after the US unleashed a new wave of strikes against Iran on Tuesday and revoked a licence allowing the country to sell oil after three tankers were attacked in the Strait of Hormuz.
Westpac analysts wrote in a research report that concerns over the stability of the peace deal had re-emerged after Iran attacked ships crossing the Strait of Hormuz.
"Concerns over the inflation outlook were in focus, seeing yields jump higher across the globe," they wrote.
Brent crude was up 2.6 per cent at US$76.12 a barrel at the start of the Asian trading session on Wednesday, extending a rally into a second day.
The New Zealand dollar was 0.1 per cent stronger at US$0.5681 ahead of a decision by the Reserve Bank of New Zealand later on Wednesday, at which it is narrowly expected to raise interest rates for the first time in more than three years.
Against the yen, the US dollar strengthened 0.1 per cent to 162.28 yen.
Bank of Japan board member Toichiro Asada, the sole dissenter to the BOJ's decision in June to raise interest rates, told Reuters on Monday he must see signs of demand-driven inflation before supporting further hikes.
The euro slipped 0.1 per cent to US$1.1405, while the British pound edged 0.1 per cent lower to US$1.3353.
The Australian dollar was steady at US$0.6926.
Bitcoin was down 0.2 per cent at US$63,518.35, while ether was 0.5 per cent lower at US$1,774.45.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: LPT3 implementation, vape issue among focus of Dewan Rakyat today
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The proposed implementation of the East Coast Expressway Phase 3 (LPT3) through a public-private partnership (PPP) model and measures to curb syndicates distributing harmful vape products targeting youths and school students are among the focus of the Dewan Rakyat sitting today.
According to the Order Paper on the Parliament's official website, during Question Time, Wan Hassan Mohd Ramli (PN-Dungun) will ask the Works Minister to state the government's key considerations for the proposed implementation of LPT3 via the PPP model, including its implications for toll rates and the project's implementation timeline.
Datuk Mas Ermieyati Samsudin (PN-Masjid Tanah) will ask the Home Minister to explain the stricter enforcement measures planned to curb syndicates distributing harmful vape products targeting youths and school students.
Also drawing attention is a question by P. Prabakaran (PH-Batu) to the Home Minister on the government's measures to address persistent congestion at the country's entry points, including efforts to expedite immigration clearance.
During the same session, Salamiah Mohd Nor (PN-Temerloh) will ask the Health Minister to state the effectiveness of digital public healthcare initiatives such as MySejahtera and electronic health records in helping to reduce congestion at government hospitals.
The Dewan Rakyat will then proceed with the winding-up debate on the Motion on the 2024 Annual Report and Financial Statements of the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) by the relevant ministers.
The Second Meeting of the Fifth Session of the 15th Parliament is scheduled to sit for 16 days and conclude on July 16. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nasdaq sinks as AI worries hit chipmakers
Article:
NEW YORK: The Nasdaq ended sharply lower on Tuesday, weighed down by losses in Micron Technology and other chipmakers due to mounting doubts about the sustainability of Wall Street's AI-driven rally.
Chip stocks in Asia and the US dropped after memory chip giant Samsung Electronics' blowout earnings report failed to satisfy investors with sky-high expectations.
Micron dropped 4.7 per cent and Sandisk lost 7.3 per cent. That helped send the PHLX chip index down 4.65 per cent, reducing its 2026 gain to about 74 per cent.
Adding to worries about high-flying chipmakers, Reuters reported that Chinese startup DeepSeek is developing its own AI chip, a push that could reduce its dependence on Nvidia and Huawei chips.
Tuesday's chip selloff marked the latest bout of volatility among memory chipmakers and other AI-related stocks as investors worry that sharp gains related to the buildout of AI data centres may have left the shares too pricey.
"The story of today is the story of the last few weeks, and that's rotation after the blistering run in the AI buildout, semis and memory.
Expectations have gotten to be almost impossible to beat for these companies," said Zachary Hill, head of portfolio management at Horizon Investments in Charlotte, North Carolina.
Another test of the appetite for chip stocks looms on Friday, when South Korean giant SK Hynix's US listing starts trading on the Nasdaq.
Elon Musk's SpaceX fell almost seven per cent in its first day of trading as part of the Nasdaq 100 index, and after a wave of brokerages initiated coverage on the stock.
Eight of the 11 S&P 500 sector indexes declined, led lower by industrials, down 3.41 per cent, followed by a 2.45 per cent loss in materials.
The S&P 500 declined 0.45 per cent to end the session at 7,503.85 points.
The Nasdaq declined 1.16 per cent to 25,818.69 points, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average declined 0.25 per cent to 52,925.15 points. The Dow briefly hit a record high earlier in the session before losing ground.
Despite the S&P 500's decline, advancing issues in the index outnumbered falling ones by a 1.3-to-one ratio.
Oil prices rose following reports of attacks on vessels near the Strait of Hormuz.
Fiserv climbed 1.8 per cent after media reports that the firm had held discussions with US banks including JPMorgan and Bank of America to sell its payments infrastructure business handling debit card transactions.
US Federal Reserve watchers will get another glimpse into how new Chair Kevin Warsh steers the central bank when it releases the minutes of its latest meeting on Wednesday, the first of his tenure.
Volume on US exchanges was relatively light, with 17.5 billion shares traded, compared with an average of 23.3 billion shares over the previous 20 sessions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: France to return Syria €50mil stolen by Assad's uncle
Article:
DAMASCUS: French President Emmanuel Macron said Tuesday during a visit to Syria that Paris will return more than 50 million euros (US$57 million) confiscated from the family of former ruler Bashar al-Assad to the Syrian people.
During his landmark visit to Damascus, the first by a European Union head of state since Assad fell in late 2024, the two countries signed several agreements focusing on development and reconstruction after more than a decade of civil war.
One of them involves the return of money that France confiscated from Rifaat al-Assad, the ousted leader's uncle.
Macron told a joint press conference with Syrian counterpart Ahmed al-Sharaa that "more than 50 million euros from the seizure of ill-gotten gains by the family of the former dictator... will be returned to the Syrian people to finance concrete development projects in the territory."
[[nid:1483224]]
The declaration of intent signed by both countries' foreign ministers said the two nations will "begin the process of returning 51 million euros from the seizure of Rifaat al-Assad's ill-gotten gains, confiscated by the French justice system."
Rifaat went into exile in 1984 after a failed attempt to overthrow then-president Hafez al-Assad, moving to Switzerland and then France, and later presenting himself as an opponent of his nephew Bashar, who succeeded Hafez in 2000.
In 2021, he returned to Syria from France to escape a four-year prison sentence for money laundering and misappropriation of Syrian public funds.
Two years later, Rifaat appeared in a family photo alongside Bashar, the ruler's wife Asma and other relatives.
Shortly after Bashar's ousting, Rifaat crossed into Lebanon and then flew out of Beirut airport, a Lebanese security source said at the time, without specifying his final destination.
His family announced his death in January, aged 88.
Rifaat's role in a February 1982 massacre during a crackdown on an armed revolt by the Islamist Muslim Brotherhood earned him the nickname "the Butcher of Hama," referring to the central Syrian city.
The death toll from the 27 days of violence, which took place under a media blackout, has never been formally established, though estimates range from 10,000 to 40,000.
Swiss prosecutors had accused Rifaat of a long list of crimes, including ordering "murders, acts of torture, inhumane treatment and illegal detentions" while serving as an officer in the Syrian army.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WCT's RM926mil Abu Dhabi contract to boost earnings over next three years: PublicInvest
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: WCT Holdings Bhd's latest contract win in Abu Dhabi is expected to provide a meaningful boost to its earnings over the next three years, said Public Investment Bank Bhd (Public Invest).
The contract, WCT's second major subcontract in Abu Dhabi, involves the construction of six residential buildings for the Yas Riva Residences project, which is being developed by Aldar Development.
The project was awarded to the 50:50 unincorporated joint venture Construction General Contracting House Ltd (CGCH)-WCT joint venture.
"Based on our estimates, this project will contribute about 14.3 per cent annually to the company's earnings over the approximately 40-month contract period," PublicInvest said in a note.
PublicInvest said that WCT's share of the work is estimated at RM463.1 million, which is expected to increase the company's outstanding construction order book by 21 per cent to RM2.2 billion.
While the firm views this job win positively, PublicInvest maintained its earnings forecasts as the job win falls within its FY2026 order book replenishment assumption of RM1 billion.
"We retain our "Neutral" call with an unchanged target price of 50 sen," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: One palm, one voice, one vision
Article:
FOR Malaysia, palm oil is not merely an industry. It is part of the country's rural backbone, export strength and national resilience. It supports estates, smallholders, mills, refiners, workers and many small businesses around them.
When palm oil is misunderstood, livelihoods, communities and a national development story are affected too.
Yet palm oil does not suffer from having too few stories. Often, it suffers from having too many stories told separately.
One voice speaks of sustainability. Another speaks of smallholders. Another speaks of certification, traceability, food security, labour, biodiversity, renewable energy and trade barriers.
Each story matters. But when they travel alone, the world hears fragments, not a common truth.
That is why palm oil needs one palm, one voice, one vision.
The industry no longer needs merely to defend itself. It must define itself. Palm oil is not just an oil in a bottle or an ingredient on a label. It is food, employment, rural income, multiplying and spin off economies, export revenue, science, technology and national resilience.
It is also a crop carrying an outsized global role from a relatively small land footprint.
Globally, oil palm occupies about 26 million hectares, less than 0.5 per cent of the world's agricultural land, yet produces more than a third of global vegetable oils.
Malaysia has around 5.7 million hectares under oil palm, a major asset for exports, livelihoods and downstream industries.
These figures are not for boasting, but for balance. A crop that delivers so much from so little land deserves fair judgement.
This does not mean palm oil should reject scrutiny. It should welcome fair scrutiny. The world is right to ask about deforestation, labour standards, biodiversity, emissions and traceability.
But fairness also means equal standards across competing oils and commodities. Too often, producers feel the finishing line keeps moving: yesterday deforestation-free, today traceable to plot, tomorrow something more demanding.
The answer is not anger. The answer is credible evidence, clearer communication and stronger solidarity.
Certification systems such as RSPO, MSPO and ISPO have helped formalise sustainability commitments.
Traceability is no longer a slogan; it is increasingly a dataset. Transparency is becoming the price of trust. Yet progress that is not understood becomes progress wasted.
This is where the industry must speak with one coherent voice. Not a propaganda voice, or one that pretends there are no problems.
But a truthful voice that says: palm oil matters, palm oil has improved, palm oil still has gaps, and palm oil is committed to closing them.
The human voice must be central. Millions of smallholders form the backbone of the crop across producing countries.
They nurture the palms, harvest the fruit and depend on the crop for household needs, education and dignity. To speak about sustainability without them is like staging a play with half the cast missing.
Solidarity among producers also matters. The Council of Palm Oil Producing Countries (CPOPC), founded by Indonesia and Malaysia and joined by other producing nations, offers a platform for a broader voice.
Used well, it can coordinate science-backed advocacy, share best practices, support smallholders and respond to unfair barriers, while showing that responsible palm oil is part of global food security, not a threat to it.
That solidarity must become a permanent habit. Labour, replanting, productivity, sustainability, market access, smallholder inclusion, research and trade barriers are too big for any company, association or country to handle alone.
If producers paddle separately, they move slowly. If they row together, they become an armada.
Palm oil also connects to oleochemicals, nutrition, biomass, biogas, sustainable fuels and rural development, all part of responsible growth and national resilience.
In the end, communication is not a campaign. It is the patient work of turning truth into trust. Palm oil has enough facts, achievements and critics. What it needs now is coherence.
For Malaysia, the task is not only to sell more palm oil. It is to tell the story better, defend it fairly, improve it honestly and share it proudly.
One palm, because the crop is shared. One voice, because the message must be clear. One vision, because the future must be built together.
*The writer has over 30 years of experience in the plantation industry, with a strong background in oil palm R&D, executive leadership and industry advocacy through MEOA and MPOA. The views expressed in this article are his own.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bomb attack rocks Damascus during Macron visit
Article:
DAMASCUS: Two bombs exploded on Tuesday near a hotel in Damascus where French President Emmanuel Macron spent the night, wounding 18 people and overshadowing the first visit to Syria by a European Union head of state since Bashar al-Assad was toppled.
Macron, whose motorcade left the hotel shortly before the blasts, pressed ahead with his visit, meeting President Ahmed al-Sharaa at the presidential palace. His office said he had not heard the blasts.
The attack underlined lingering security challenges facing Sharaa, a former al Qaeda commander who has built close ties with Western states as he has sought to rebuild a country shattered by 13 years of civil war.
FRANCE READY TO HELP ON SECURITY, ECONOMY
The explosions struck a busy area of Damascus between the Tourism Ministry and the national museum across the street from the Four Seasons hotel, where a source in Macron's delegation and Syrian security sources said he had spent the night and had met civil society groups on Tuesday morning.
Posting on X just after the blasts, Macron said his visit continued and praised the "dignity, courage and determination" of Syrians he had met.
"We are not naive about the risks, but they are being managed," Macron said later in a news conference with Sharaa. "Certain groups" sought to prevent "Syria's full and complete reintegration into the international community," he added.
Macron also said France was working to redefine its security and military cooperation with Syria, including the potential support of French special forces to fight Islamic State, which has claimed several attacks on Syrian forces this year.
There was no immediate claim of responsibility for Tuesday's attack. Sharaa said investigations were ongoing.
Macron, who led calls for the lifting of Western sanctions on Syria last year, was accompanied by business leaders, including the CEOs of TotalEnergies and shipping group CMA CGM. He said France was ready to help rebuild Syria's economy and banking sector.
The Elysee said CMA CGM signed a partnership deal with Syria, including air cargo freight handling at Damascus airport, and that France and Syria would start a process to restore to Syria €51 million (US$58.3 million) of assets confiscated from the late Rifaat al-Assad, Bashar's uncle.
TotalEnergies' CEO said his company would discuss signing an offshore exploration contract with Syrian officials, but that lingering insecurity meant a return to onshore oil activities was still not a viable option.
FLAMES AND BILLOWING SMOKE
The first blast hit soon after Macron's motorcade left for the presidential palace. Reuters footage showed flames and smoke billowing from the site when a second explosion was caught on camera a few metres away.
[[nid:1483213]]
The second blast went off next to an ambulance parked at the scene, where some two dozen people had gathered.
Reuters video showed Macron's motorcade heading along a highway towards the presidential palace before the blasts.
Islamic State, an adversary of Sharaa during the civil war, declared a new phase of operations against his government in February.
Aron Lund of the Century International think-tank said such attacks could dent confidence in Syria's recovery, but they posed no threat to government control over the country.
"It's a worrying phenomenon, but I don't think we should overstate it. It's been 1-1/2 years and Islamic State hasn't re-emerged in the way many feared," he said.
DAMASCUS CAFE BOMBED LAST WEEK
The Syrian Interior Ministry said security forces had identified the bombs and were preparing to defuse them when they exploded.
The bombs — one placed in a car parked on the roadside and the other in a trash can — were planted outside a security cordon around Macron's place of residence and posed no threat to his visit, the ministry said.
Internal security forces have launched search operations to identify those responsible, it said.
The French Presidency said the blasts were not audible from the presidential motorcade, and a Reuters journalist with the press group accompanying Macron did not hear them.
Last week, a bomb at a Damascus cafe killed nine people and wounded 20 others. There was no claim of responsibility.
Sharaa, a member of Syria's Sunni Muslim majority, has pledged to build an inclusive new order in Syria since ending more than five decades of iron-fisted rule by the Assad family.
But his promise has been tested by bouts of violence pitting pro-government forces against members of religious and ethnic minority groups, with many hundreds killed last year.
France is currently represented in Syria by a charge d'affaires who is not fully present in Damascus. Macron said both countries would appoint ambassadors, whom Sharaa said would be exchanged as soon as possible.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia overhauls auto incentives to deepen local supply chain [WATCH]
Article:
RAWANG, Selangor: The government is refining its New Customised Incentive Mechanism (NCIM) to ensure investments in the automotive industry deliver stronger domestic industrial capabilities while accelerating the growth of the local supply chain.
Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani said the enhanced incentive framework forms part of Malaysia's broader strategy to position itself as a gateway to the Asean market of more than 700 million people.
It is placing greater emphasis on strengthening domestic industries, particularly small and medium enterprises (SMEs).
Johari said the revamped NCIM would be simplified to improve clarity for investors while prioritising high-value activities such as engineering, software development, electronics and battery technologies.
Malaysia's domestic market may comprise only around 34 million people, but its strategic advantage lies in serving as a regional automotive manufacturing and assembly hub for the wider Asean market, he told reporters after launching the MCE Auto Hub here today.
The government's long-standing commitment to developing the automotive industry was never solely about vehicle production, Johari said.
It is also about building a robust manufacturing ecosystem that creates quality jobs, nurtures local suppliers and expands the country's automotive component manufacturing capabilities.
He said the refined incentive mechanism would reinforce these objectives by encouraging investments that generate greater value for the domestic economy and strengthen Malaysia's position in the regional automotive value chain.
"Each vehicle consists of around 25-30 major components. Not every component needs to be manufactured locally, but those that are suitable should be produced by local companies to strengthen the supporting industry."
He acknowledged that local automakers such as Proton Holdings Bhd and Perusahaan Otomobil Kedua Sdn Bhd have grown through partnerships with international technology partners.
Johari welcomed foreign investors but stressed that such investments must benefit the domestic economy by developing the industrial ecosystem and increasing local content.
"Investors should not come merely to sell vehicles. They must be committed to building the industry in Malaysia, involving local SMEs and helping to develop the nation's supply chain," he said.
Johari is targeting a gradual increase in local content utilisation, starting at around 10 per cent before rising up to 40 per cent, in line with the industry's capabilities.
He said Malaysia's automotive components industry has grown significantly, with about 730 specialised automotive vendors supporting an ecosystem that employs more than 700,000 people and contributed RM84 billion to the country's gross domestic product in 2025.
However, he cautioned that Malaysia's motor vehicle trade deficit, including parts and components, had widened considerably over the same period, increasing from RM13 billion in 2020 to RM33.8 billion in 2025.
In this regard, he said the government is formulating a new mechanism that is simpler and more transparent to encourage high-quality investments, including improving the methodology for calculating local content and refining investor incentives.
"The government hopes to implement the initiative as early as next year once the policy enhancement process has been finalised.
"Malaysia will also continue to leverage its position within Asean as a key competitive advantage for investors, positioning the country as a manufacturing and export hub for the regional market of more than 700 million consumers.
"In addition to attracting foreign investment, the government also emphasises the importance of protecting and strengthening the capabilities of local SMEs so that the benefits of economic growth can be enjoyed more broadly," he said.
Meanwhile, Johari said the automotive industry landscape has changed rapidly over the past few years, particularly following the emergence of Chinese vehicle manufacturers and the rapid growth of electric vehicles.
"Without a strategic and forward-looking approach, there is a real risk that the domestic market will become increasingly dominated by imported products, placing greater pressure on the local supplier ecosystem," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pakistan-registered cargo plane missing off Karachi coast after navigation problem
Article:
MEXICO CITY: A Pakistan-registered Boeing 737 cargo plane with five crew members on board lost contact with air traffic control on Tuesday night after reporting a navigational system problem on its way from Sharjah to Karachi, Pakistan aviation authorities said.
Early flight data indicated the 27-year-old converted freighter operated by K2 Airways possibly crashed into the sea southwest of Karachi after a series of sharp altitude changes before a steep final descent, according to flight-tracking service Flightradar24.
Authorities have launched a coordinated search and rescue operation at sea through various agencies to locate the missing plane, the Pakistan Airports Authority said on Facebook.
The plane reported a navigational system issue at 9.18pm Pakistan Standard Time (1618 GMT) while flying towards Karachi, the airports authority said.
Local air traffic control tried to guide the aircraft, but three minutes later radar systems showed the plane descending rapidly and communication was lost, the authority said.
The flight was about 155 nautical miles (287km) west of Karachi at the time, according to the statement.
The aircraft went missing while flying over the Arabian Sea near Ormara in Balochistan, Pakistan, local media Geo News reported.
Flightradar24 tracking data showed chaotic final minutes as the plane plunged about 5,000 feet in less than a minute, surged back some 6,000 feet in just 30 seconds, before a catastrophic dive from 36,550 feet.
The last transmitted data point placed the aircraft at 1,100 feet above sea level, with a vertical rate of minus 22,400 feet per minute – about 400 kilometres per hour – an extremely steep and abnormal rate of descent.
"Anytime you see something extreme like that, it catches your eye, but it is too soon to say what any of it means without more information," said Anthony Brickhouse, an aerospace safety consultant.
The missing aircraft is part of Boeing's decades-old 737 family but is two generations older than the 737 MAX version that was involved in a recent safety crisis.
It uses engines made by CFM International, jointly owned by GE Aerospace and France's Safran.
The 737-400 was first delivered as a passenger plane to Russia's Aeroflot in 1999 and was converted to a freighter in 2012, according to Flightradar24.
It is K2 Airways' only aircraft and entered into service with the carrier in 2024.
K2 Airways and Boeing did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
If casualties are confirmed, the incident would be the first fatal crash in Pakistan since 2020, when a Pakistan International Airlines Airbus A320 came down short of the runway in Karachi, killing 97 people after pilots were distracted discussing the coronavirus before a failed landing attempt.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US launches new strikes on Iran after reinstating oil sanctions over shipping attacks
Article:
TEHERAN: The US military unleashed a new wave of strikes against Iran on Tuesday and revoked a licence allowing the country to sell oil after three tankers were hit in the Strait of Hormuz, putting pressure on an already fragile ceasefire.
After a day in which huge crowds mourned Iran's slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in the holy city of Qom, US Central Command announced that it had begun a series of strikes intended to impose what it called heavy costs.
"Iran's demonstrated aggression was unwarranted, dangerous, and a clear violation of the ceasefire," Centcom said on X.
Iranian media reported explosions early Wednesday local time in the southern port city of Sirik, on Qeshm Island, and in Bandar Abbas.
The reports said strikes hit a commercial pier in Sirik and fishing piers in both Sirik and Bandar Abbas.
A US official told Reuters that strikes targeted Iranian air defence systems, coastal surveillance systems, surface-to-air missiles, anti-ship cruise missiles and drone launch sites.
The incidents were only the latest threat to the ceasefire agreement the US and Iran struck last month, pausing the conflict that started in February with US and Israeli strikes across the Islamic republic.
In a potentially major blow to that agreement, Washington moved on Tuesday to withdraw a key concession that had allowed Iran to sell oil on international markets.
Oil prices rose more than 3 per cent after the US announced the move.
A US official said earlier negotiators continued to work in good faith towards a final agreement with Iran.
But control of the strait has given Teheran immense leverage, effectively allowing it to force a stalemate with the world's most powerful military.
Analysts say Teheran uses attacks on ships to underscore that leverage as it negotiates a long-term peace deal with the US.
Under the interim US-Iran agreement, the US Treasury issued a June 22 general licence to allow the sale of crude oil and petrochemical and petroleum products of Iranian origin through Aug 21.
In revoking that licence on Tuesday, it gave Iran until July 17 to wind down any transactions.
Iran's foreign ministry condemned the move as a breach of the framework agreement to end the war and said Washington would bear responsibility for the consequences.
The ministry said early on Wednesday that Iran would take any measure it deemed necessary to safeguard its interests and national security.
Qatar blamed Iran for attacking the vessels, including the huge Qatari liquefied natural gas tanker, the Al Rekayyat, which reported being struck overnight by a drone that caused a fire in its engine room.
The crew were safe and being evacuated.
A Saudi-flagged crude oil tanker, believed to be the supertanker Wedyan, was also damaged off Oman, maritime security sources said.
The cause was not immediately clear.
Qatar's foreign ministry said it had summoned Iran's deputy ambassador and handed him a protest note following the attack on the tanker.
Iran's foreign ministry said Qatar's accusations were perplexing and that Teheran was diligently fulfilling its commitments but asserted that commercial vessels faced risks for using routes not coordinated with Iran.
A second US official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said initial indications were that Iran had fired at three commercial vessels.
UNDREDS OF THOUSANDS TAKE TO STREETS IN QOM
Iran's clerical rulers aim to install a permanent system to collect fees in what would amount to a huge shift in the balance of power in a region where Washington has long acted as guarantor of security.
At home, the leadership has used the mourning for Khamenei that began last week to show its control after Khamenei was killed with his daughter, granddaughter, son-in-law and daughter-in-law on the war's first day.
The caskets of the slain leader and family were driven through the streets of the seminary city of Qom on Tuesday, where many hundreds of thousands of people carried flags and banners comparing Khamenei to revered Shiite martyrs.
In chants they vowed to avenge Khamenei.
Some bore placards and banners reading "K*** Trump."
Later on Tuesday, Iranian state media showed what it said was footage of an aeroplane carrying Khamenei's coffin at the airport of the Shiite holy shrine city of Najaf in neighbouring Iraq.
TRUMP: 'MAKE A DEAL OR WE'RE GOING TO FINISH THE JOB'
The ceasefire was intended to provide a 60-day window for negotiations on a permanent agreement, but indirect talks in Qatar ended last week with no sign of headway.
US President Donald Trump has repeatedly threatened to resume bombing, most recently on Monday when he told reporters in the Oval Office: "We're either going to make a deal or we're going to finish the job... We can knock down their bridges in one hour, we can knock out their energy supply."
Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said that under the terms of the interim ceasefire memorandum, negotiations on the final deal would "not commence if threats continue".
In launching the war four months ago, Trump said his aims were to destroy Iran's nuclear and missile programmes, end its ability to threaten its neighbours and create conditions for Iranians to topple their leaders.
None of those goals has been met, although Washington says a permanent deal will halt what it says is an Iranian programme that could make a nuclear bomb, which Iran says it never sought.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Beijing is looking at curbing overseas access to China's top AI models
Article:
SINGAPORE: Chinese authorities have held meetings with top tech firms over the past month about potentially restricting overseas access to China's most advanced AI models, including those yet to be released, three people familiar with the discussions said.
The talks follow a number of steps by Beijing to keep homegrown AI within the country and underscore how China, like the US, is now treating cutting-edge artificial intelligence as a critical national asset that needs controls.
Companies present at the talks included tech giants Alibaba and ByteDance as well as startup Z.ai, said the people, who were not authorised to speak to the media and declined to be identified.
Since the emergence of DeepSeek's R1 model last year, Chinese AI models have made big inroads globally thanks to their low costs and increasing capabilities. Any decision by Beijing to limit access to those products could ripple across AI markets as costs for many businesses would likely increase.
TOUGHENING UP PENALTIES FOR AI THEFT DISCUSSED
At the meetings, led by China's Ministry of Commerce, participants discussed putting limits on the most advanced AI models – both closed-source and more open versions, according to two of the sources.
Officials talked about making any leak or theft of proprietary AI technology an offence under China's stringent national security law, one of the sources said.
The officials also raised the possibility of implementing new measures to restrict who can fund domestic AI startups, the source added.
The scope of the potential restrictions is still being discussed, two sources said, adding that they may only apply to future models. It was not immediately clear when or even if they would come into force.
China's commerce ministry, which oversees export regulations, and the National Development and Reform Commission – the country's state planning agency whose officials also attended the meetings – did not respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Alibaba, ByteDance and Z.ai also did not respond to Reuters queries.
All three companies have a range of AI models, some closed-source while others are open-weight, meaning users can download, run and customise the underlying systems.
Alibaba's Qwen and ByteDance's Doubao are two of the most widely used AI models in China. Z.ai has recently set Silicon Valley abuzz as the capabilities of its GLM-5.2 model come close to leading US offerings but at a fraction of the cost.
AI MODELS AND NATIONAL SECURITY CONCERNS
US President Donald Trump's administration has also been deeply concerned about national security implications of AI – in particular the potential for American AI products to be misused by military intelligence in China, Russia and other countries of concern.
In June, it ordered that foreign nationals not have access to Anthropic's most advanced Fable and Mythos models, which prompted the company to disable the models for all users globally as nationality could not be verified in real time.
Export controls for Fable, which is designed for the general public, have since been lifted after new safeguards were put in place. But Mythos, designed for cybersecurity professionals, is still only available to some "trusted" US organisations.
Some US AI experts have also said the US needs to regulate the use of Chinese AI models.
ANGST IN CHINA ABOUT MYTHOS THREAT
According to two of the sources, Chinese authorities are deeply worried about the potential for Mythos to exploit software vulnerabilities and that Washington might deploy the model against Chinese interests.
That echoes concerns publicly voiced by state media and Zhou Hongyi, founder of cybersecurity firm 360, a major vendor to government and enterprise clients, who has said China needs to develop its own Mythos.
China this year has implemented numerous measures to protect homegrown AI.
In April, the country's state planner ordered Meta to unwind its US$2 billion acquisition of Chinese-founded AI startup Manus. In early June, authorities issued sweeping new rules, tightening control of overseas deals that involve Chinese investors, technology, data and national security.
China had also launched investigations this year into Manus and other local AI startups that had moved abroad, seeking to establish whether they have broken export control laws, according to two of the sources and a third person.
Manus has not responded to requests for comment.
Reuters was not able to learn how any potential new restrictions on overseas access to Chinese AI models might work.
But some hints might be gleaned from a May roundtable of Chinese legal experts on regulations governing open-source AI.
According to a summary of the discussions published in an official Supreme People's Court journal, participants proposed a tiered system: basic open-source tools subject to a simple filing, more advanced technologies facing security reviews, and the most sensitive frontier models barred from public release or restricted to domestic use.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor election: Parties battle for decisive swing seats
Article:
JOHOR BARU:    With polling just days away, parties contesting the Johor election have identified constituencies they consider safe, difficult to wrest from rivals or too close to call.
Campaign efforts have now shifted towards a final push to sway voters in several marginal seats that could determine the scale of victory this Saturday.
Political observers said at least 44 of the 56 seats have   clear   political inclinations, and parties are concentrating their final campaign efforts  on the  handful of marginal constituencies where the outcome remains uncertain.
Of the 44 seats, between 29 and 30 seats are expected to favour Barisan Nasional.
Universiti Teknologi Malaysia Faculty of Social Sciences and Humanities Associate Professor Dr Mazlan Ali said BN would have the upper hand in these seats, where Malays form the largest group of voters.
"BN has no major concerns about its chances in Malay-majority seats, where  it has traditionally enjoyed strong support.
"Of these 29 to 30 seats, 23 are not being contested by Perikatan Nasional, which will further improve BN's chances of winning these constituencies," he said.
Among them are seven seats considered BN's safest constituencies — Machap, Buloh Kasap, Tanjung Surat, Larkin, Benut, Pulai Sebatang and Kukup.
Machap is among the key seats being watched in this election, with caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi seeking a second term in  the constituency.
He faces Pakatan Harapan's Nur Hafiz Roslan, who has 18 years of experience in the legal field.
Other seats considered safe for BN include Sri Medan, Parit Raja, Semarang, Rengit, Mahkota, Kahang, Endau, Tenggaroh, Panti, Pasir Raja, Sedili, Johor Lama and Penawar.
In Sri Medan, for example, BN has retained Parit Sulong Umno  chief Datuk Zulkurnain Kamisan to defend the seat. Better known as "Tok Jun", his popularity in the constituency is regarded as difficult to challenge.
It is a  similar situation  in Parit Raja, where BN has fielded   Nor Rashidah Ramli, who had held the seat for two terms. The Sri Gading Umno deputy  chief has the advantage of being well known locally and is seen as more familiar to voters than her PN and PH challengers.
Ayer Hitam Umno  chief Datuk Samsolbari Jamali, who has represented Semarang for the past five terms, is also expected to comfortably defend his seat.
Former health minister Datuk Seri Dr Adham Baba's return to contest Pasir Raja is also seen as strengthening BN's prospects in what has long been regarded as an Umno fortress.
A BN election machinery worker  painted an even more optimistic picture, saying the coalition had identified 35 safe seats and was intensifying efforts in at least five other constituencies in its push for a landslide victory.
"We are focusing on these seats at the moment. If all goes well, we are likely to secure all 40. If we work hard enough, we may be able to add one or two more seats to our tally," he said, without revealing the seats.
BN is contesting all 56 seats in the election, as is its federal government partner, PH.
Observers said PH is likely to win seats where Chinese voters form the majority, as well as several mixed constituencies.
In Johor, there are 14 seats where Chinese voters form the largest voter group.
In the 2022 state election, PH component DAP won 10 of these constituencies —  Jementah, Tangkak, Bentayan, Penggaram, Mengkibol, Johor Jaya, Stulang, Perling, Skudai and Senai. The party, however, lost Bekok and Yong Peng to BN's MCA.
The other Chinese-majority constituencies are Puteri Wangsa and Bukit Batu. The latter is considered a risky seat for PH after the coalition won it in the previous state election by a razor-thin majority of just 137 votes.
The situation is different in Puteri Wangsa, where PH is seeking to reclaim the seat from Muda. The constituency was traditionally held by PH before Amanah handed it to Muda in the last election.
For this election, PH is fielding former education minister Dr Maszlee Malik.
However, the entry of Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli's party Bersama means PH may not have an easy path to victory in the seat.
"Rafizi has his influence and we cannot deny that. His party may reduce the majority secured by PH, but it is still likely that PH will win the seat.
"As this is the first time Bersama is contesting an election, it remains uncertain how many votes from PKR, DAP or MCA supporters will shift towards Bersama,"  Mazlan said.
Mazlan, however, said voter turnout was likely to influence the final outcome. A turnout of below 60 per cent may favour BN, but a turnout of between 60 and  70 per cent could give PH a chance to create an upset or deny BN a landslide victoru.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ukrainian capital Kyiv under missile attack, two injured
Article:
KYIV: Ukraine's capital Kyiv came under a Russian missile attack early on Wednesday, triggering fires and injuring at least two people, Mayor Vitali Klitschko said.
Klitschko, writing on Telegram, said strikes in the capital had caused a fire in a storage area and a non-residential building in two areas on either side of the Dnipro River.
Two people were injured, with one requiring treatment in hospital.
The air alert lasted for about an hour.
A missile strike in the southern port of Odesa earlier in the evening injured 10 people, regional governor Oleh Kiper said. Eight were being treated in hospital.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Banting school stabbing: Victim's father denies bullying claims
Article:
BANTING:    The father of the Form 3 sudent who was stabbed in the SMK Bandar Banting incident on Monday denies that his daughter is a bully, as alleged by  unverified notes circulating online.
Mohd Firuz Ibrahim said his daughter was a quiet student who rarely socialised outside of her family circle.
"I want to emphasise that my daughter is not a bully.
"She is a quiet student who keeps to herself.
"She is always chaperoned," he said.
Firuz had  earlier said his daughter's name was No. 10 on a list of 12 believed to be the suspect's targets.
However, he  stressed that the list, part of purported notes being circulated online, had not been verified.
One of the notes highlighted alleged incidents of bullying at the school.
The author of the  notes    accused students on the list  of bullying and mocking her history of self-harm.
The writings reveal  a desire to "study with friends" and  expressed frustration over being kept away from school by her psychiatrist.
The  author also expressed regret to her mother,   apologising for the "trouble" and emotional distress caused by her ongoing mental health struggles.
The victim is   in stable condition following a three-hour surgery to treat multiple wounds, including a punctured lung.
At press time, the    Education Ministry has yet to respond to the
New Straits Times'
enquiries on the notes   and if a  probe into the bullying allegations was underway.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Banting school stabbing: Suspect allegedly hunted for specific targets [WATCH]
Article:
BANTING:     Students who witnessed Monday's rampage at SMK Bandar Banting said the  knife-wielding suspect appeared to be hunting for certain individuals before stabbing the only  victim.
A Form Four student said the suspect, who is   a former student, entered the   school during recess  between 9.30am and 10am.
"She came in and maybe the security guard didn't realise she had already been suspended.
"She acted as though she was looking for specific people... she chased many of us but only managed to get one," the student said, adding that the suspect appeared to   target female students.
Another student, a Form Three boy, said the victim was the suspect's best friend.
The victim, also a Form Three student,   is being treated  in hospital for multiple stab wounds.
He said the attack had shocked everyone   and students were struggling to understand what had driven the suspect to violence.
He added that on the morning of the stabbing, the suspect reportedly posted an "outfit check" on her Instagram account, wearing the same clothes seen during the attack.
"She had already made a list of the clothes she would wear and on which day, the names of those she planned to attack, and how she was going to carry it out," he said.
Students said calm had returned to the  school.
"It feels normal today. We didn't expect this to happen at our school, but we understand it's an isolated case," said the Form Three boy.
"The ones who are most traumatised are the girls. Most of the students she chased were girls rather than boys."
Students fled as teachers rushed to bring the situation under control before police arrived and arrested the suspect.
Meanwhile, police were tight-lipped about handwritten notes circulating on social media  allegedly linked to the suspect.
The notes  contained plans for  an  attack, as well as possible dates for doing so.
The authorities have yet to verify the authenticity of the notes.
Kuala Langat police chief Superintendent Mohd Akmalrizal Radzi told the
New Straits Times
that   Selangor police chief Datuk Shazeli Kahar would issue updates on the case.
At press time, Shazeli has not responded to enquiries about the purported notes.
Meanwhile, the victim's father, Mohd Firuz Ibrahim, said his daughter's name was on the purported  list of targets circulating online.
"It looks like my daughter's name is there at No. 10," the 43-year-old said, adding that he did not wish to speculate further.
Firuz said it remained unclear whether the document was written by the suspect, obtained from another source or fabricated. He has given his statement to police.
The suspect has  been remanded to assist in investigations.
Firuz said his daughter's condition was stabilising, but she would have to remain in hospital for at least a week.
He said his daughter had yet to speak about the attack and was still shaken.
"She is still crying, so I'm letting her rest for now. I don't want to ask her anything," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'AI, digital tools can help popularise, preserve calligraphy'
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
ARTIFICIAL intelligence (AI), digital platforms and creative media can be harnessed to preserve and popularise calligraphy among the younger generation.
National Unity Minister Datuk Aaron Ago Dagang said digitising calligraphy works, organising virtual exhibitions and developing learning applications through technological collaborations could help preserve the traditional art.
He said embracing innovation would allow tradition and modern technology to coexist, enabling calligraphy to evolve with the times while preserving its authenticity.
"Calligraphy possesses values that cannot be replaced. It must be preserved and recognised as a living cultural heritage," he said at the opening of the Asean Book Fair and Unity Calligraphy Festival 2026 here on Monday.
Present was Yang di-Pertua Negeri Tun Ramli Ngah Talib.
Aaron said the Unity Calligraphy Festival 2026 celebrated the Jawi, Chinese, Tamil and other traditional scripts as they reflected the cultural identity of Malaysia's diverse communities.
He added that the festival, which was first held last year, also served as a platform to foster greater appreciation of the different writing traditions while promoting understanding, tolerance and mutual respect among the people.
On reading, he said Malaysians read an average of 24 books annually, a significant increase from two in 2005 and 15 in 2015.
He said 88.6 per cent of Malaysians had developed a reading habit, with the average reader consuming 16 printed books and eight digital books annually, reflecting a balanced preference for both formats.
Aaron said his ministry, through the National Library of Malaysia, had expanded access to reading materials via the u-Pustaka digital library, which now offers more than one million digital titles.
He added that printed books continued to enjoy strong popularity, citing the record attendance of more than 2.4 million visitors at this year's Kuala Lumpur International Book Fair.
"The government remains committed to making Malaysia a reading nation by 2030 through continued collaboration with publishers, writers, educational institutions and the public."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cybersecurity law review may introduce whipping, victim compensation
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
The government is reviewing cybersecurity laws, including the possibility of introducing whipping for offenders and victim compensation.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the Legal Affairs Division was conducting an in-depth study on providing comprehensive protection for cybercrime victims.
"It is looking closely into the issue, covering cybercrime, online harms and digital offences," she said after attending the National Cyber Security Summit 2026 here yesterday.
"Unlike in Singapore where whipping is imposed on offenders, we only have fines and imprisonment. This is what we will study."
Azalina added that the current domestic legal framework focused on punitive action against perpetrators under the Penal Code and Criminal Procedure Code, while victim protection still required closer scrutiny.
On online scam cases, she said in the United Kingdom and Australia, banks involved would refund the victims but "our country does not have such a mechanism".
"Through this study, we want to look at other countries' mechanisms, specifically regarding the types of punishment and remedies for victims.
1.26 "Most of our laws now only punish the perpetrators. The victims can lodge a report but sometimes, they do not even get their money back. That is an element we want to review," she added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: S. Korea trade mission secures RM58m in sales
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: A market immersion programme in South Korea, jointly organised by the Malaysia External Trade Development Corp (Matrade) and Mara, generated more than RM58 million in sales for participating Malaysian companies.
The programme in Seoul, held from June 29 to July 1 under Mara's Gate to Global initiative, connected Malaysian firms with South Korean companies.
The programme is part of a collaboration between Matrade and Mara following a memorandum of understanding signed in November 2025.
The partnership aims to strengthen Malaysian companies' export readiness and support overseas expansion.
Five Malaysian companies took part in the mission.
They showcased capabilities in artificial intelligence safety inspection solutions, software and artificial intelligence services, data center evaluation services, remotely operated vehicle services and smart mapping technologies.
During the mission, participants met South Korean companies through business matching sessions, company visits and market engagement activities organised by Matrade's Seoul office.
Matrade said the programme helped Malaysian companies gain insights into South Korea's business environment, industry trends and customer requirements while identifying opportunities for market entry and collaboration.
Its chief executive officer, Abu Bakar Yusof, said South Korea offers opportunities for Malaysian companies with innovative products and specialised services.
He added that the results show the ability of Malaysian companies to compete overseas and reflect the appeal of Malaysia's technology-based solutions.
Matrade and Mara said they will continue working together to strengthen Malaysian businesses' competitiveness through export promotion initiatives, strategic partnerships and expansion programmes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NGO urges whole-school safeguarding approach after school stabbing in Banting
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 8 — The Education Ministry has been urged to strengthen and implement the Safe School Policy more comprehensively across all educational institutions following the recent stabbing incident at a school in Banting, Selangor.
